text: `cat /etc/cron.d/nightly-sync` -- the line itself looks completely correct: valid schedule, valid user, valid command. Look past the visible content.

Check whether the file actually ends with a newline character: `tail -c 1 /etc/cron.d/nightly-sync | od -c` (or simply notice your shell prompt appears right after `cat`'s output with no line break before it).

cron requires every crontab line, including the last one, to be newline-terminated -- if the file doesn't end in `\n`, cron treats that final line as incomplete and silently drops it. No error is logged anywhere; it's simply never scheduled.
Fix it by appending a newline: `echo >> /etc/cron.d/nightly-sync` (an empty `echo` just appends `\n`). Re-check with `od -c` that the file now ends in `\n`.
